subject: mathematics

sections.title: Angles of Isosceles and Equilateral Triangles

prose_summary:
Section 9.1 defines congruence and then reasons about which measurements are enough to recreate a figure exactly. It opens with the practical problem of recreating a signboard symbol on another board: tracing the outline on tracing paper works for small figures but is impractical for big ones, so the section asks what measurements would let us reproduce the figure. Naming the corner points A, B, C, it shows that the two arm lengths AB and BC alone do not fix the symbol — with AB = 4 cm and BC = 8 cm, several different symbols can be built from the same two lengths because the angle between the arms is free to vary. Adding the included angle ∠ABC = 80° does fix the shape and size, so those three measurements produce an exact replica. From this the section states the central definition: figures that are exact copies of each other, having the same shape and size, are congruent, and congruent figures can be superimposed exactly one over the other. It stresses that while checking for congruence a figure may be rotated or flipped before superimposing, so rotated or flipped pairs can still be congruent. The section then returns to the two-symbol verification question and reasons that if both symbols have the same arm lengths and the same included angle we can be sure they are congruent, whereas equal arm lengths alone are not enough. Its Figure it Out set asks students to check given pairs, circle the pairs that appear congruent, and decide what measurements are needed to reproduce a circle or a rectangle and hence how one would test whether two circles, or two rectangles, are congruent.

Section 9.2 justifies the standard triangle-congruence conditions through construction and reasoning, then applies them to identify and express congruence. Framed by Meera and Rabia needing to replicate a triangular frame too big to trace, it works case by case. Measuring the three sidelengths (40, 60, 80 cm, scaled to 4, 6, 8 cm to fit the page) leads to Rabia's construction — a base segment of one length with arcs of the other two lengths swung from its endpoints — which meets at two points forming ∆ABE and ∆ABF; by tracing, taking cut-outs and superimposing, or observing that AB acts as a line of symmetry, these are shown congruent, establishing that equal sidelengths guarantee congruence: the SSS (Side Side Side) condition. The section then develops the conventions for writing congruence (∆ABC ≅ ∆XYZ), explaining that the order of vertices in the two names encodes the corresponding vertices, sides, and angles, so ∆ACB ≅ ∆XYZ would be incorrect while ∆ACB ≅ ∆XZY is a valid restatement; a rectangle split by a diagonal is used to practise finding the correct correspondence via the common side. It next examines the angle-only case (30°, 70°, 80°), showing that equal angles give the same shape but not the same size, so AAA does not guarantee congruence. Two sides and the included angle give the SAS condition, which does guarantee congruence, while two sides and a non-included angle (SSA) are shown by a two-arc construction to permit two non-congruent triangles ∆PQR and ∆PQS — so SSA fails in general. Two angles and the included side give the ASA condition (congruence guaranteed), illustrated further by a figure in which O is the midpoint of AD and BC, where the vertically opposite angles complete an SAS argument. Two angles and a non-included side are reduced to ASA using the fact that a triangle's angles sum to 180° to recover the third angle, giving the AAS condition. Finally, for right triangles, equal hypotenuse and one leg give the RHS condition, a special case in which SSA does guarantee congruence, shown by a perpendicular-and-arc construction. The section closes by listing the sufficient conditions — SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS — and its several Figure it Out sets apply them to determine, justify, and express congruence and to deduce equal parts, parallelism, and angle bisection from congruent triangles.

Section 9.3 proves the angle properties of isosceles and equilateral triangles using congruence as the tool. Starting from an isosceles triangle with AB = AC and ∠A = 80°, it constructs the altitude from A to the base BC, meeting it at D, and shows ∆ADB ≅ ∆ADC by the RHS condition (equal sides AB and AC as hypotenuses, the common side AD, and the right angles at D), from which ∠B = ∠C follow as corresponding parts of congruent triangles. This establishes the general result that in any triangle the angles opposite equal sides are equal. Applying the same fact twice to an equilateral triangle — AB = AC gives ∠B = ∠C, and AB = BC gives ∠A = ∠C — shows all three angles are equal; since they must add up to 180°, each equals 60°. The section emphasises that these facts have been deduced purely from the notion of congruence, invites students to verify them by construction, and displays congruent triangles in real structures such as the Louvre pyramid in Paris, the Egyptian Pyramid of Giza, dome and rangoli designs, and the Howrah Bridge. Its concluding Figure it Out set asks students to read a congruence statement (∆AIR ≅ ∆FLY) for its corresponding parts, test five measurement cases for congruence with reasons, prove parallelism from congruent triangles, show that a square's diagonal splits it into triangles congruent in several different ways, and find missing angles from segments marked with equal ticks.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 9.1 p.3
enumerated_exercises.description: Check if the two given figures are congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 9.1 p.3
enumerated_exercises.description: Circle the pairs of figures that appear congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 9.1 p.3–4
enumerated_exercises.description: State what measurements you would take to create a figure congruent to a given (a) circle and (b) rectangle, and use this to state how you would check whether two circles, and two rectangles, are congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 9.1 p.4
enumerated_exercises.description: Decide how to check whether two figures of the given type are congruent, and use the method to identify whether each of the following pairs is congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 9.2 p.8
enumerated_exercises.description: Suppose ∆HEN is congruent to ∆BIG. List all the other correct ways of expressing this congruence.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 9.2 p.8
enumerated_exercises.description: Determine whether the given triangles (sides 6 cm, 5 cm, 3.5 cm) are congruent. If yes, express the congruence.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 9.2 p.9
enumerated_exercises.description: Given AB = AD and CB = CD, identify any pair of congruent triangles and explain why, and decide with reasons whether AC divides ∠BAD and ∠BCD into two equal parts.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 9.2 p.9
enumerated_exercises.description: Given DF = DG and FE = GE, decide whether ∆DFE and ∆GED are congruent to each other.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 9.2 p.13
enumerated_exercises.description: Identify whether the triangles (marked 7 cm, 47°, 5 cm) are congruent, state which condition you used, and express the congruence.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 9.2 p.13–14
enumerated_exercises.description: Given that CD and AB are parallel and AB = CD, find the other equal parts in the figure (using equal alternate angles) and, if the two triangles are congruent, express the congruence.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 9.2 p.14
enumerated_exercises.description: Given ∠ABC = ∠DBC and ∠ACB = ∠DCB, show that ∠BAC = ∠BDC and decide whether the two triangles are congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 9.2 p.14
enumerated_exercises.description: Identify the equal parts in the figure, given that ∠ABD = ∠DCA and ∠ACB = ∠DBC.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q1, section 9.3 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Given ∆AIR ≅ ∆FLY, identify the corresponding vertices, sides, and angles.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q2, section 9.3 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Each case (a)–(e) gives measurements from two triangles; identify the pairs that are congruent, with reason, and express the congruence whenever they are congruent.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q3, section 9.3 p.20
enumerated_exercises.description: Given OB = OC and OA = OD, show that AB is parallel to CD (using AD as a transversal and equal alternate angles).

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q4, section 9.3 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: ABCD is a square; show that ∆ABC ≅ ∆ADC and decide whether ∆ABC is also congruent to ∆CDA. Give more examples of triangles congruent in two different ways, and an example of two triangles congruent in six different ways.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q5, section 9.3 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: Find ∠B and ∠C if A is the centre of the circle and the marked angle at A is 120°.

enumerated_exercises.book_ref: Figure it Out Q6, section 9.3 p.21
enumerated_exercises.description: Find the missing angles in the figure, using the convention that segments marked with a single tick are equal to each other and those with a double tick are equal to each other.
